name: Bloom Energy Corporation

profile.description: Bloom Energy Corporation manufactures and deploys solid oxide fuel cell systems (Bloom Energy Servers) that generate clean, reliable, on-site electricity from natural gas, biogas, or hydrogen. In FY2025, Bloom achieved record revenue of $2.02B (+37.3% YoY) driven by AI data center power demand, with $14B in service backlog. Management guides $3.1B–$3.3B in FY2026 revenue (+54-63% YoY) with $425M–$475M in non-GAAP operating income.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Bloom's solid oxide fuel cell technology enables on-site power generation that bypasses grid infrastructure — precisely the capability AI data centers require when grid capacity and reliability are constrained[2]. CEO KR Sridhar explicitly frames Bloom as the answer to the 'AI intelligence factory power demand' that cannot tolerate grid disruptions[2]. The product backlog has more than doubled YoY, Bloom is scaling manufacturing to 2GW/year capacity by end-2026, and the Electricity-as-a-Service model (EaaS, zero-upfront through Brookfield partnership) removes customer CapEx barriers to adoption[1].

inspiration.label: STRONG
inspiration.analysis: Founder-CEO KR Sridhar's mission — 'making clean, reliable energy affordable for everyone on Earth' — is 25 years deep, rooted in NASA Mars exploration research, and substantiated by Bloom's unique ability to operate on natural gas, biogas, or hydrogen[2]. The Inspire score of 66 (ALIGNED) is the highest in this batch, with 22 positive ESG attributions including Air Quality, Critical Risk Management, Environmental Risk Mitigation, and GHG Emissions[2]. Former GE CEO Jeff Immelt and former Cisco CEO John Chambers on the board bring enterprise scaling credibility.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Q4 2025 revenue of $777.7M (+35.9% YoY) beat estimates by $130M, FY2025 revenue was $2.02B with adjusted EBITDA of $271.6M[1]. Non-GAAP gross margin was 30.3% (+1.6pts YoY) and the service business achieved 20% gross margin for the 8th consecutive profitable quarter[1]. The company holds $2.45B in cash but carries a significant debt load, and Q4 non-GAAP gross margin of 31.9% declined from Q3's 39.3% — a volatility that constrains the infrastructure score.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: Bloom Energy is the only publicly-traded pure-play company specifically positioned to solve the AI data center power crisis — AI is the primary demand driver for its product[2]. Sridhar explicitly positions Bloom as the 'power plant for the AI intelligence factory' in public statements, and the product backlog doubling YoY directly reflects AI data center buildout[1]. The company cannot be disrupted by AI; it is disrupted by AI NOT growing, which appears highly unlikely through 2030.

infinite_game.summary: Bloom Energy's 25-year mission to make clean energy affordable globally, KR Sridhar's founding conviction from NASA research, and the company's long-horizon investment in manufacturing scale and the EaaS business model all reflect genuine infinite-game orientation. The $14B service backlog creates a decades-long revenue runway.

just_cause.analysis: Making clean, reliable energy affordable to every person on Earth — eliminating energy poverty while addressing climate change — is one of the most genuinely transformative just causes in the public markets, held consistently for 25 years by founder CEO KR Sridhar[2].

trusting_teams.analysis: COO Satish Chitoori (scaling manufacturing to 2GW) and CCO Aman Joshi (data-center-first commercial strategy) represent a stable operational team under Sridhar's founder-CEO vision, supported by high-profile board governance[2].

worthy_rivals.analysis: Bloom competes with natural gas utilities, independent power producers, and grid-scale battery solutions for data center power; it positions its reliability and on-site generation as genuinely superior to utility alternatives for mission-critical AI workloads[2].

existential_flexibility.analysis: Pivoting from clean energy startup to AI data center power infrastructure provider, launching the EaaS model with Brookfield, and advancing hydrogen capability demonstrate consistent willingness to reinvent the go-to-market while maintaining platform technology consistency[1][2].

courage_to_lead.analysis: Sustaining the clean energy mission through 20+ years of startup losses, the COVID disruption, and two CEO transitions at key board positions — while doubling manufacturing capacity at the precisely right moment — requires extraordinary founder conviction[2].

investment_thesis: Bloom Energy's bull case is that it is the only publicly traded pure-play AI data center power infrastructure company — a category that should command premium valuation multiples given AI hyperscaler capex commitments of $200B+ in 2025-2026[1][2]. The $3.1-$3.3B in 2026 revenue guidance (+54-63%) with $14B in service backlog and 2GW manufacturing scale-up by end-2026 provide rare revenue visibility in a high-growth company[1]. The Inspire score of 66 and 22 positive ESG attributions make Bloom uniquely positioned for ESG-mandated institutional capital alongside growth capital.
thesis_continued: The bear case is gross margin volatility and valuation execution risk: Q4 non-GAAP gross margin fell from 39.3% (Q3 2025) to 31.9%, the 2026 guidance range of $425M-$475M operating income is wide (11% spread), and the stock has already surged 500%+ in the past year — pricing in a significant portion of the AI power thesis[1][2]. D/E of 125% and still-negative net income (-2.2% profit margin) mean Bloom remains highly vulnerable to execution misses. At 28.5x forward earnings on still-negative GAAP income, any revenue shortfall vs. the $3.1-$3.3B guidance would cause rapid multiple compression.
key_catalysts: Q1 2026 revenue confirming the $3.1B+ annual run rate is on track, with non-GAAP gross margin stabilizing above 32% as the higher-cost inventory cycles through
2GW manufacturing capacity achieved by end-2026 as guided, enabling Bloom to capture the full backlog opportunity without supply chain constraints
Hydrogen-capable Bloom Server commercial launch for large industrial customers, opening a $50B+ addressable market beyond AI data centers
key_risks: FY2026 revenue falling below $2.8B (30%+ short of guidance midpoint) if AI data center power purchase decision timelines lengthen, triggering a 40-50% stock correction from the 500%+ run
Quarterly gross margin volatility below 28% for two consecutive quarters as higher-cost inventory and customer mix issues persist, signaling the business model cannot sustain the 32% target
Natural gas price spike above $6/MMBtu reducing the economic advantage of Bloom Energy Servers vs. grid alternatives, slowing the AI data center customer adoption rate
sources: Bloom Energy Q4 2025 Earnings Release — investor.bloomenergy.com, February 5, 2026
Bloom Energy CEO KR Sridhar on AI Power Strategy — Semafor, September 2025; The AI Power Gap Deep-Dive — FinancialContent, March 23, 2026
Bloom Energy Record Revenue and 2026 Outlook — AlphaSpread, February 2026
